coredata.dc:title: Template-free microwave-assisted fabrication of carbon dots/Zn(OH)2 composites for separation and enhancing chemical sensing
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Hou, Juan
Zhou, Tianyu
Wang, Long
Zhang, Ping
Ding, Lan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, Zn(OH)2 nanosheets embedded with carbon dots (CDs) were prepared through a template-free microwave-assisted route for the first time. The preparative strategy was facile, green and cost-effective. The final composites possessed uniform size distribution, good fluorescence property, excellent stability and fast adsorption ability for metal ions. A novel and ultrasensitive fluorescent assay has been proposed using CDs/Zn(OH)2 composites as amplifying fluorescent sensors for rapid detection of Cu2+ and Hg2+ in real water samples. Aided by a Cu2+/Hg2+ chelating agent, exclusive detection could be expected without cross-reactivity, as well as reversible and reproduced analysis. The detection limits were 0.3nM for Cu2+ and 0.2nM for Hg2+, respectively. This assay could effectively enrich/separate and detect Hg2+ and Cu2+ simultaneously, revealing remarkable advantages including operational simplicity, high sensitivity, low cost and acceptable reproducibility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Microwave irradiation
Zn(OH)2 nanosheets
Fluorescence detection
Heavy metals

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the mechanism for the determination of Cu2+ and Hg2+ by CDs/Zn(OH)2 sensor.
Different magnifications TEM images (A–D) and SEM images (E and F) of CDs/Zn(OH)2 composites.

(A) XRD pattern of CDs/Zn(OH)2 composites, sharp diffraction peaks marked by “●” can be indexed to Zn(OH)2; (B) FT-IR spectrum of CDs/Zn(OH)2 composites; (C) TGA curves of CDs/Zn(OH)2 composites in N2 and air atmosphere.
XPS spectrum (A), and high resolution XPS spectrum of Zn 2p (B), O1s (C) and C 1s (D).

Photographs of CDs/Zn(OH)2 dispersed in water under visible light (A) and under UV light (B); images of Cu2+ solution (C), CDs/Zn(OH)2-Cu2+ before centrifugation (D) and after centrifugation (E); (F) fluorescence emission spectra of CDs/Zn(OH)2 (blue line), CDs/Zn(OH)2-Cu2+ (green line), CDs/Zn(OH)2-Hg2+ (pink line), CDs/Zn(OH)2-Cu2+-EDTA (black line) and CDs/Zn(OH)2-Hg2+-GSH (red line), 400nM Cu2+ and Hg2+ were used. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Fluorescence spectra of CDs/Zn(OH)2 with indicated concentrations of Cu2+ (A) and Hg2+ (B) in PBS (10mM, pH 7.0), error bars=±standard deviation, n =3.

Effects of common ions (A) and molecules (B) on the fluorescence of Zn(OH)2/CDs alone, (SMM: sulfamonomethoxine; Trim: trimethoprim, Tc: tetracycline hydrochloride; Cef: ceftriaxone; FF: florfenicol; St: sym-triazine; Val: valine; Iso: isoleucine; Thr: threonine; Ala: alanine; Pro: proline; Leu: leucine; Gly: glycine; Tyr: tyrosine; Phen: phenylalanine); Influence of coexisting common ions (400nM) on the Zn(OH)2/CDs-Cu2+ (400nM) system (C) and Zn(OH)2/CDs-Hg2+ (400nM) system (D), (error bars=±standard deviation, n =3).

head.ce:title: Template-free microwave-assisted fabrication of carbon dots/Zn(OH)2 composites for separation and enhancing chemical sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CDs/Zn(OH)2 composites were firstly prepared by a template-free microwave-assisted route and used for the separation and detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ simultaneously.

In this work, Zn(OH)2 nanosheets embedded with carbon dots (CDs) were prepared through a template-free microwave-assisted route for the first time. The preparative strategy was facile, green and cost-effective. The final composites possessed uniform size distribution, good fluorescence property, excellent stability and fast adsorption ability for metal ions. A novel and ultrasensitive fluorescent assay has been proposed using CDs/Zn(OH)2 composites as amplifying fluorescent sensors for rapid detection of Cu2+ and Hg2+ in real water samples. Aided by a Cu2+/Hg2+ chelating agent, exclusive detection could be expected without cross-reactivity, as well as reversible and reproduced analysis. The detection limits were 0.3nM for Cu2+ and 0.2nM for Hg2+, respectively. This assay could effectively enrich/separate and detect Hg2+ and Cu2+ simultaneously, revealing remarkable advantages including operational simplicity, high sensitivity, low cost and acceptable reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal pollution has become a severe environmental concern. Among the heavy metals, mercury ions (Hg2+) can cause serious diseases, such as brain damage, mitosis impairment and kidney failure, even at a low concentration [1,2]. Copper ions (Cu2+) may lead to adverse effects on the central nervous and immune systems [3,4]. Many quantitative methods for Hg2+ and Cu2+ have been developed, including X-ray absorption spectroscopy [5], surface enhanced Raman scattering [6], colorimetric analysis [7,8] and electrochemical analysis [9,10], etc. However, those techniques often suffer from complicated sample pretreatment, low sensitivity, long-time operation procedures and/or specialized skills.

Fluorescent sensing platform has become a powerful methodology due to its rapid response, operational simplicity and real-time detection [11]. Various nanomaterials have been applied to the detection of heavy metal ions, including Ag/Au nanoclusters [12–15], CdS quantum dots [16,17], CdTe quantum dots [18,19], CdSe quantum dots [20,21], ZnS quantum dots [22], etc. Nevertheless, these nanomaterials often trigger potential environmental hazards caused by heavy metals. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have drawn increasing attention due to their superior optical properties and promising applications in sensing, bio-imaging and drug delivery [23]. Most CDs-based sensors for heavy metal ions are of a “one-to-one” readout type, severely limiting their advanced applications in routine analysis [2,3]. To retain their physical stability and avoid secondary pollution, CDs have been encapsulated in cyclodextrin [24], molecular imprint polymers [25], and metal-organic frameworks [26], etc. As a consequence, both sensitivity and selectivity are enhanced significantly. Despite the merits above, organic reagents requirement, time-consuming preparation procedures and difficulty in surface functionalization are the drawbacks. In view of detection cost and material utilization, it is desirable to fabricate high-efficiency nanomaterials by a simple and controllable method in order to meet demands for simultaneous removal and detection of different metal ions.

Generally, nanostructured divalent metal hydroxides, such as Cd(OH)2, Mg(OH)2, Cu(OH)2 and Zn(OH)2, have been investigated and used as precursors or sacrificial templates to generate corresponding metal oxides [27,28]. Zn(OH)2 is usually utilized to prepare ZnS, ZnSe and ZnO [29–31], but its adsorption/separation performance has been few reported.

Herein, we designed a template-free and cost-effective route to introduce guest fluorescent CDs into Zn(OH)2 nanosheets. The preparation could be completed in a short time owing to the intensive and homogeneous microwave irradiation. The whole process did not require special equipment, and the experimental conditions were facile to control. A self-assembly growth mechanism was proposed to explain the formation of CDs/Zn(OH)2 composites. The as-prepared composites could be employed as separation/enrichment and enhancing sensing materials for Cu2+ and Hg2+ in either online or off-line modes. Scheme 1 demonstrates the mechanism of the experiment. The composites could strongly accumulate metal ions due to the superior adsorption and separation performance of Zn(OH)2. Hg2+ and Cu2+ could interact with the organic groups on the surface of CDs and quench the fluorescence of the composites. With the addition of ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA) and glutathione (GSH), Cu2+ and Hg2+ could be masked, which ensured the selective and repeated determination. Compared with original CDs sensor without the amplifying effect of Zn(OH)2, the proposed sensor showed much lower detection limits (LODs). It is the first time to establish a separated/enriched and sensitive sensing platform based on CDs/Zn(OH)2 composites, which will open an avenue for the sensitive analysis of metal ions and offer new opportunities for the applications of CDs.

Zn(NO3)2∙6H2O was purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NaOH and citric acid were obtained from Tianjin Jinko Institute of Refined Chemical Engineering. Other chemicals were obtained from Beijing Chemicals (Beijing, China). Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2MΩ cm was obtained from the United States Milli-Q water purification system. Standard stock solutions of Cu2+/Hg2+ were prepared with CuSO4/HgCl2 in ultrapure water. All the solvents and chemicals were of analytical grade without further purification.

CDs were prepared from citric acid according to our previous report [32]. Briefly, citric acid (5g) and disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4) (1g) were dissolved in 10mL of ultrapure water. The mixture was stirred for 5min and then heated in a 750W microwave oven for 2min 30s. The obtained CDs powders were purified with ethanol-water solution (V:V, 80:20), separated by centrifugation and dried at 60°C.

Then, CDs (0.15g) and ZnNO3·6H2O (1.50g) were dissolved in 25mL of ultrapure water under mild stirring for 10min. NaOH aqueous solution (0.2M) was added dropwise with constantly stirring for 20min until the pH was adjusted to 6.5. Subsequently, the mixture was heated in a microwave oven at 225W for 25min. After cooling down to room temperature, 0.76g of CDs/Zn(OH)2 composites (CDs content: ∼19.7%) were collected by centrifugation, washing with water several times and drying at 45°C. Finally, the composites were dispersed in water for use.
As a control, Zn(OH)2 was also prepared in the same manner without the addition of CDs.

Morphology images of the composites were photographed by JEM-2100 transmission election microscope (TEM) at an accelerating voltage of 200kV and JEM-6700F scanning electron microscope (SEM) operated at 10.0kV. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded using a Shimadzu XRD-6100 spectrometer. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were collected by using a Nicolet 360 FT-IR spectrophotometer equipped with a DGTS detector. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was recorded by a TG209F1 thermal analysis system. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were observed on a Nanoscope scanning probe microscope from Digital Instruments. The fluorescence lifetime and fluorescence quantum yield were recorded on the Edinburgh Instrument (FLS 920). All fluorescence measurements were carried out in a 1cm path-length quartz cuvette on a Shimadzu RF 5301 PC spectrophotometer. The slit widths of excitation and emission were set at 10nm (see details in Supporting information).

A typical procedure for fluorescence determination was performed as follows. The stock solution of CDs/Zn(OH)2 composites was diluted in phosphate buffer solution (PBS: pH 7) with a final concentration of 240mgL−1. Then, 100μL of CDs/Zn(OH)2 stock solution and different amounts of Cu2+ or Hg2+ standard solution were added into the quartz cuvette. The mixture was then adjusted to 2.0ml with water and shaken thoroughly. After 20min of equilibration at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra were recorded by setting the excitation wavelength at 260nm.

Tap water samples were collected from our laboratory and dormitory, respectively. The water samples were filtered through 0.22μm membranes to remove particulate matters. Prior to fluorescence detection, different concentrations of Hg2+ or Cu2+ were added to prepare the spiked samples. The concentrations of Hg2+ and Cu2+ in water samples were obtained by standard addition method.

Fig. 1 displays TEM and SEM images of CDs/Zn(OH)2 composites at different magnifications. SEM images clearly demonstrate that the composites are made up of thin sheets with sizes ranging from 100–200nm. AFM image indicates a uniform surface with thickness of 5.4±0.4nm (Fig. S1). High-resolution TEM image in Fig. S2 verifies a well-defined crystallized structure with inter-planar spacing of 0.25nm and 0.32nm, ascribing to the basal spacing of Zn(OH)2 and graphite, respectively [29,32]. Compared to Zn(OH)2 obtained in the same manner (Fig. S3), the composites exhibit much smaller sizes and a more uniform distribution. Small particle size and thin layer structure are beneficial to fast adsorption performance and good dispersity in water, making the composites applicable for chemical/biological sensors.

Fig. 2 A shows the XRD pattern of the composites. The strong and sharp diffraction peaks marked by “●” can be indexed to Zn(OH)2, which further indicates that the materials are well crystallized [29]. The broad diffraction peak around 26° for graphite species is assigned to CDs [32]. Compared to the XRD pattern of Zn(OH)2 (Fig. S4A), the composites demonstrate highly developed orientation of exposed crystal faces.

The FT-IR spectrum is shown in Fig. 2B. The IR absorption band at around 3512cm−1 represents OH groups and water ligands. Strong peak at 1720cm−1 is due to the CO groups. The characteristic peaks at 2916cm−1 and 2851cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of sp2 and sp3 C-H, further indicating the presence of CDs in the composites [4]. The characteristic peaks at 1602cm−1 and 1368cm−1 are attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of COOZn [28]. The peaks at 1754cm−1, 1012cm−1 and 758cm−1 are assigned to υ(OH), δ(OH) and γ(OH) [33]. Peaks below 1000cm−1 represent the combination of ZnO vibrations [30].

Fig. 2 C shows TGA curves of CDs/Zn(OH)2 composites conducted in a flowing air (red curve) and N2 atmosphere (black curve), respectively. The black curve shows a first weight loss of 9.1% in the range of 0–250°C due to the removal of water intercalated into the nanosheets. A second weight loss of 20.5% in the range of 250–800°C corresponds to the dehydration of chemically bound CDs and the decomposition of the framework. The experimentally determined weight loss in air atmosphere (44.5%) is higher than that in N2 atmosphere (30.2%) due to the loss of C content in air, demonstrating the existence of CDs in the framework.

XPS scan of the composites verifies the presence of C (14%), O (44%) and Zn (42%) as well as limited H without other impurities (Fig. 3 and Table S1). The binding energy peaks at 285.0, 531.0, 1022.7 and 1045.6eV correspond to C1s, O1s, Zn2p2/3 and Zn2p1/2, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s shows four distinct peaks. The binding energy peak at 283.87eV confirms the graphitic structure (sp2 CC) of CDs. Peaks at 284.79 and 285.69eV suggest the presence of CC (sp3) and CO (sp3), respectively [4]. Peak located at 288.80eV is attributed to OCO/ZnCO (sp2). For the high-resolution spectrum of O1s, three peaks at 531.47, 532.43 and 533.35eV are assigned to CO, OZn and CO bands, respectively [33,34]. The high-resolution spectrum of Zn2p3/2 reveals the presence of ZnO (1022.2eV) and ZnC (1023.5eV) [33]. The surface components of the composites observed by XPS are in good agreement with those obtained by FT-IR analysis.

The synthetic conditions were investigated in detail in the supporting information (see Experimental section of the ESI† for details). Upon the analysis in section 3.1, a possible preparation mechanism is thus proposed. In this work, microwave irradiation could provide homogeneous and intensive heating due to the ionic conduction effect, which significantly promoted the reaction rate and led to uniform size distribution [32,34].

The carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs were good ligands for Zn2+ and could coordinate with Zn2+. With time, the release of Zn2+ from Zn2+ (COOH) n complexes took place, and thus the concentration of free Zn2+ in the solution was efficiently controlled in this manner. The self-assembly growth was similar to the preparation of 3D flowerlike Cd(OH)2 reported previously [28,35]. However, the complexation slow-release in this work did not require extra organic templates and ligands (such as tartaric acid and hexadecylamine) [35,36]. Furthermore, surface-located hydroxyl groups on Zn(OH)2 and the carboxyl groups of CDs would generate hydrogen bonds, which afforded the composites growth along specific directions [37]. Thus, the as-prepared composites exhibited more uniform distribution and highly developed orientation. Therefore, it is speculated that the use of CDs has benefit for the growth of the products with uniform size distribution.

The pH value has great influence on the rate of ionic motion and coordinating interaction, eventually determining the growth of final composites [36]. When the pH was higher than 6.5, the composites tended to form bulk structure. When the pH was lower than 6.5, ZnO nanospheres were prepared instead.

Water could limit the reaction temperature at its boiling point. In this open system, the vaporization of water molecules could remove excessive heat. In turn, microwave irradiation replenished the heat loss. So the reaction started and continued at the boiling point of water (100°C, measured by a thermocouple), which effectively avoided the conversion of Zn(OH)2 into ZnO. Meanwhile, the mixture did not require stirring due to the aid of boiling bubbles.

Overall, the preparation of CDs/Zn(OH)2 composites was controlled by a series of conditions, including a relatively low reaction temperature with an upper limit, continuous heating, bubbles and structural direction.

Fig. S5 gives the photos of Zn(OH)2 and CDs/Zn(OH)2 powders under a visible light and a UV beam, suggesting strong fluorescence emission from the composites. CDs show strong fluorescence emission at 440nm when excited at 350nm, whereas the composites show strong fluorescence emissions at 295nm and 390nm when excited at 260nm (Fig. S6). It is speculated that Zn2+ and COOH on the surface of CDs might generate an electric field, which leads to the recombination of trapped charge and the change of surface states [38]. The absolute quantum yield was measured to be 2.3% using a fluorimeter and an integrating sphere. As shown in Fig. S7, there are no discernible changes in both peak characteristics and fluorescent intensities within 90min under a UV beam (365nm), as confirmed by observed 2% relative standard deviation (RSD) for 11 repeated detections. Besides, the composites can be stable for at least 15 days.

Fig. 4 (F) shows that the fluorescent intensities of CDs/Zn(OH)2 decrease obviously in the presence of Hg2+ or Cu2+, demonstrating that Hg2+ and Cu2+ can effectively quench the fluorescence. The fluorescence of CDs/Zn(OH)2 quenched by Cu2+ and Hg2+ reached equilibrium at approximately 16min and 14min, respectively (Fig. S8). Therefore, fluorescence measurements were monitored after the addition of Cu2+ or Hg2+ for a fixed time of 20min.

The pH value of the solution could affect not only the fluorescent intensities and stability of the composites, but also the subsequent fluorescence quenching behavior by Hg2+ and Cu2+. The composites were stable in the range of pH 6–8. Strong acidic or basic media would damage the structure of layered Zn(OH)2. We investigated the pH effect on the quenched fluorescence efficiency (Effq) in the pH range of 6–8 (Fig. S9). The Effq was calculated by Eq. (1):
(1)
E f f
q
( % )
=
(
F
0
− F )
F
0
where F 0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities of CDs/Zn(OH)2 at 390nm in the absence and presence of Cu2+/Hg2+, respectively. When Hg2+ was added, the Effq increased with increasing the pH from 6.0 to 7.0 and then decreased. When Cu2+ was added, the Effq gave the best results at pH 6.5. Such pH-dependent fluorescence behavior was ascribed to the reaction between metal ions and CDs, as well as the adsorption/desorption equilibrium between metals ions and the composites. At pH 7.0, the Effq of Hg2+ and Cu2+ both kept at relatively high values. In order to simplify the experimental procedure, pH 7.0 was chosen in the further experiment, which was also suitable for the application in environmental water samples.

The fluorescence spectra of CDs/Zn(OH)2 in the presence of a series of Cu2+ concentrations are illustrated in Fig. 5 A. The fluorescent intensity of the composites decreased gradually with increasing the concentration of Cu2+ and no shift of spectral shape was observed. The inset reveals the dependence of (F0-F)/F0 on the Cu2+ concentration in the range of 0–600nmolL−1 with the following regression equation:
(2)
(
F
0
− F )
F
0
= 0.00101
( ± 0.00002 )
C
Cu
2 +
+ 0.003
( ± 0.0005 )
(
R
2
= 0.9964 )
, n mol
L
− 1
where F 0 and F are the fluorescent intensities of CDs/Zn(OH)2 in the absence and presence of Cu2+, respectively. The LOD for Cu2+ was calculated to be 0.3nM based on the ratio of signal-to-noise of 3 (see details in Supporting Information, Fig. S10A). It is lower than the maximum allowable concentration of Cu2+ in drinking water permitted by the World Health Organization (WHO, 32μM).

Then, the fluorescence response of the sensor to Hg2+ was also investigated (Fig. 5B). Hg2+ can be determined over the linear concentrations in the range of 0–400nM, with the regression equation:
(3)
(
F
0
− F )
F
0
= 0.00164
( ± 0.00001 )
C
Hg
2 +
+ 0.002
( ± 0.0003 )
(
R
2
= 0.9978 )
, nmol
L
− 1
The LOD for Hg2+ was 0.2nM (Fig. S10B) and much lower than the maximum allowable level of Hg2+ in drinking water permitted by WHO (30nM).

Herein, Cu2+ or Hg2+ was injected into CDs/Zn(OH)2 stock solution and original CDs solution with similar fluorescent intensities (Fig. 5 and Fig. S11). The CDs/Zn(OH)2 sensing platform is much more sensitive than the CDs probe towards the detection of Cu2+ and Hg2+. To determine the nature of the quenching process, the fluorescence lifetimes were also carried out. From two-exponential law of the fluorescence decay curves, no discernible changes were observed after the addition of Hg2+ and Cu2+ (Fig. S12). Thus, the quenching process was static in nature. In this work, the composites demonstrated strong adsorption ability to Hg2+ and Cu2+, which could accumulate the analytes around CDs. Hg2+ and Cu2+ might interact with the organic groups on the surface of CDs. Stable metal complexes were formed on the surface of CDs, leading to the specificity and intrinsic sensitivity of quenching performance based on fluorescence resonant energy transfer [39,40]. Strong metal ion-adsorption ability of the composites could synergistically quench the fluorescence, thereby leading to a significant enhance in the sensing sensitivity. The comparison of the proposed sensor and other reported data for Cu2+ or Hg2+ determination are listed in Tables S2 and S3.

Precision and reproducibility are essential properties for practical applications. To reproduce the sensor, the composites were stirred for 5min with EDTA solution and GSH solution, respectively. The fluorescent intensity of CDs/Zn(OH)2 composites could be restored almost completely (Fig. 6 A). EDTA could bind to Cu2+ with its two amines and four carboxylates and the fluorescence of the composites was restored nearly to the original value, while few effects on the fluorescence were observed for that with Hg2+. According to previous reports, different interactions might occur between metal ions and the composites [12]. In the presence of GSH, Hg2+ could be detached from CDs/Zn(OH)2 composites by strong binding preference between the thiol groups and Hg2+ [11]. Aided by the chelating agents, the CDs/Zn(OH)2 sensor was able to be reused for at least 7 repeated analyses (Fig. S13). The RSD of the sensor from the same batch and different batches were 3% and 4% at 5nM Cu2+ level, 2% and 5% at 5nM Hg2+ level, respectively.

Finally, the selectivity of the proposed sensing platform was assessed by testing other tolerance ions and common compounds. As shown in Fig. 6A and B, only Cu2+ and Hg2+ can trigger the significant and immediate quenching of the composites. The results in Fig. 6C and D demonstrated that the CDs/Zn(OH)2-Hg2+/Cu2+ system was relatively less influenced by other physiological ions. With the addition of EDTA or GSH, selective detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ could also be expected without cross-reactivity.

Therefore, the proposed sensing platform based on CDs/Zn(OH)2 composites shows ultra-high sensing sensitivity, good selectivity, satisfactory reproducibility and specific ability for sensing Cu2+ and Hg2+.

The applicability of CDs/Zn(OH)2 as efficient adsorbents for Cu2+ and Hg2+ was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4C–E, after the addition of CDs/Zn(OH)2 composites into the blue Cu2+ solution and centrifugation of the mixture, almost all the Cu2+ were adsorbed and the supernatant was nearly colorless. Subsequently, the same amounts of CDs/Zn(OH)2 composites were respectively added into a 10ppm solution of Cu2+ and Hg2+ under stirring for 12h to reach adsorption/desorption equilibrium. After centrifugation, the supernatants were analyzed by ICP/ICP-MS. The net ion removal efficiencies were 90±1% for Cu2+ and 83±2% for Hg2+, respectively. The composites demonstrated good operative efficiency for the separation of Cu2+ and Hg2+.

In order to validate the feasibility and practicality of the proposed method in real samples, the developed sensing platform was applied to the determination of Cu2+ and Hg2+ in two tap water samples from our laboratory and dormitory. The concentrations of Hg2+ and Cu2+ in the samples were derived by the standard addition method. The results are summarized in Table S4 and consistent with the values obtained by ICP/ICP-MS analysis. The recoveries of Cu2+ and Hg2+ were in the range of 92% to 104% and the RSD was lower than 5%. The above data demonstrated the potential applicability of the CDs/Zn(OH)2-based fluorescence sensor for the determination of Cu2+ or Hg2+ in environmental water samples (Table S4).

In short conclusion, we have presented a template-free microwave-assisted method to encapsulate CDs in layered Zn(OH)2 nanosheets for the first time. The preparative route was green, rapid, simple and low-cost. The composites not only exhibited excellent fluorescence properties, but also could effectively separate and accumulate heavy metal ions due to good adsorption properties of the composites. Such CDs/Zn(OH)2-based fluorescence sensor has been further applied to the label-free determination of Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions with low detection limits and broad detection ranges. Moreover, the addition of EDTA or GSH could successfully mask Cu2+ or Hg2+, which also allowed the reproducible and selective detection of Cu2+ and Hg2+. This ultrasensitive sensor has been successfully applied to the real water samples analysis with satisfactory results. It is envisioned that this sensing platform is promising in the enrichment/removal and detection of heavy metals in either online or off-line modes.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.02.122.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

Anal. Chim. Acta

Chem. Mater.
Novel hollow microspheres of hierarchical zinc–aluminum layered double hydroxides and their enhanced adsorption capacity for phosphate in water
J. Hazard. Mater.
A novel one-pot route for large-scale preparation of highly photoluminescent carbon quantum dots powders
Nanoscale
Self-assembly in an evaporating nanofluid droplet: rapid transformation of nanorods into 3D fibre network structures
Soft Matter
Microwave-assisted synthesis of wavelength-tunable photoluminescent carbon nanodots and their potential applications
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces
Hydrothermal self-assembly of hierarchical cobalt hyperbranches by a sodium tartrate-assisted route
RSC Adv.
A versatile cooperative template-directed coating method to synthesize hollow and yolk-shell mesoporous zirconium titanium oxide nanospheres as catalytic reactors
Nano Res.
Tracking the structural dynamics of hybrid layered double hydroxides
Chem. Mater.

Appl. Surf. Sci.

Anal. Chem.
Composite QDs@MIP nanospheres for specific recognition and direct fluorescent quantification of pesticides in aqueous media
Anal. Chem.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Juan Hou is currently carrying out her graduate work for her Doctor’s degree under the guidance of Professor Ding in Jilin University. Her major research areas focus on the synthesis and functionalization of carbon quantum dots and their applications on sensors for pollutions in environmental and food samples.

Tianyu Zhou is currently carrying out her graduate work for her Doctor’s degree under the guidance of Professor Ding in Jilin University. Her major research areas focus on the synthesis and applications of multi-functional absorbents.

Long Wang is currently carrying out his graduate work for his Doctor’s degree under the guidance of Professor Ding in Jilin University. His major research areas focus on the synthesis and bio-applications of heteroatom-doped carbon quantum dots.

Ping Zhang is currently carrying out her graduate work for her Master’s degree under the guidance of Professor Ding in Jilin University. Her major research areas focus on the application of microwave technology in analytical chemistry, pertaining to the preconditioning of sample use.

Lan Ding is a professor at the Department of Analytical Chemistry at the College of Chemistry, Jilin University. She received her doctor degree from Jilin University (China) in 2000 and her post-doctor degree from Harbin Institute of Technology (China) in 2003. Her research focuses on the preconditioning of sample use and application of microwave technology in analytical chemistry.
